title: Functions, Callbacks, Context, and Memory Interfaces · C Memory Workbench
summary: A guided implementation pair receives a clean input trace and acceptance examples, then builds the first working typed callback and context registry while narrating each size, lifetime, ownership, and cleanup decision. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of typed callback and context registry while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Declare and call function pointers with exactly compatible prototypes and return conventions.
Bind callback, context pointer, ownership, thread, and unregister lifetime into one interface contract.
Inject matched allocate, resize, and release operations with context and failure semantics.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Commit to the mission model · Workshop Function Pointer Compatibility
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop native-systems team handles memory case 3077. Produce bounded C evidence to bind callback results to explicit context lifetime; change the size, lifetime, failure, or ownership condition; print the observed result and invariant. Instant code stays inside the PicoC practice subset. C23 syntax, current GCC and Clang, optimization, ABI, threads, sanitizers, analyzers, fuzzers, operating-system resources, foreign runtimes, load, and production behavior require named controlled transfer gates.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Commit to the mission model · Workshop Function Pointer Compatibility.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for declare and call function pointers with exactly compatible prototypes and return conventions. For Commit to the mission model · Workshop Function Pointer Compatibility, keep Function Pointer Compatibility connected to executable C memory invariant and explicit native transfer evidence.

At Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Api Contract Documentation, trace this concrete input and predict size arithmetic, pointer range, lifetime transition, allocation result, cleanup order, printed output, and native evidence still missing before reviewing it: A workshop native-systems team handles memory case 6723. Produce bounded C evidence to bind callback results to explicit context lifetime; change the size, lifetime, failure, or ownership condition; print the observed result and invariant. C23 syntax, current GCC and Clang, optimization, ABI, threads, sanitizers, analyzers, fuzzers, operating-system resources, foreign runtimes, load, and production behavior require named controlled transfer gates. stimulus.title: Api Contract Documentation failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: A descriptive function name is a sufficient memory contract.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Document nullability, bounds, aliasing, mutation, ownership, lifetime, errors, and thread rules at declarations.

options.text: For test an acceptance boundary, run one convenient Api Contract Documentation case, omit size, bounds, lifetime, owner, allocation failure, cleanup, invariant output, and native transfer gates, then call the browser result production-safe. This does not satisfy Api Contract Documentation for typed callback and context registry.
Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Api Contract Documentation condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.

Keep the plausible Api Contract Documentation output and hide the violated typed callback and context registry assumption instead of explaining the failure. That claim fails the test an acceptance boundary evidence boundary for Api Contract Documentation.

steps.title: Defend the transferred model · Workshop Api Contract Documentation
steps.instruction:
Write the workshop evidence note for Api Contract Documentation: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A workshop native-systems team handles memory case 5104. Produce bounded C evidence to bind callback results to explicit context lifetime; change the size, lifetime, failure, or ownership condition; print the observed result and invariant. Instant code stays inside the PicoC practice subset. C23 syntax, current GCC and Clang, optimization, ABI, threads, sanitizers, analyzers, fuzzers, operating-system resources, foreign runtimes, load, and production behavior require named controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Document nullability, bounds, aliasing, mutation, ownership, lifetime, errors, and thread rules at declarations.

Evidence target: Defend the transferred model · Workshop Api Contract Documentation.
steps.why: Retrieving Api Contract Documentation through the concrete typed callback and context registry case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide. For Defend the transferred model · Workshop Api Contract Documentation, keep Api Contract Documentation connected to executable C memory invariant and explicit native transfer evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Document nullability, bounds, aliasing, mutation, ownership, lifetime, errors, and thread rules at declarations.
